segments.text: Now we come to Nadesvita, 3.7.65, this is the famous Kalamasvuta.
It's not that difficult to tell, but it's a bit long.
One occasion, the exalted one, while going is round among the coastal lands with a great
company of monks, came to Kesaputa, the district of the coastal lands.
Now the Kalamas of Kesaputa heard it, then go to Mother, recluse the Sakinskla, son, who
went forth as a one-drill from the Sakinskla and had reached Kesaputa, and this was noise
abroad about good amount that the exalted one is an exalted one, Arahan, Julien,
Lighten one, perfect in knowledge and practice, etc., etc., and it will indeed a good
thing to get sight of such Arahans.
So the Kalamas of Kesaputa came to see the exalted one, and reaching him some salute to
the exalted one and sat down at one side, some greeted the exalted one, etc., etc., etc.
And as they thus set the Kalamas of Kesaputa set this to the exalted one, certain recluses
and bramines come to Kesaputa, as to their own view they proclaim and expound it in full,
but as to the view of others the abusive, reviled, depreciated, and crippled it, or
over the other recluses and bramines on coming to Kesaputa do likewise, when we listen
to them, we have doubt and we bring as to which of these were these, the speaking
truth and we speak, suppose, would I just talk here to comment, this happens very often,
but people listen to monks, they have different monks, different opinions, so in the same way,
even the Buddhist time, people were confused, so they asked the Buddha what to do, and
they have different opinions from different monks, so the Buddha replied, yes Kalamas,
you may well doubt, you may well wave, in a doubtful matter, we bring as a rise, now look
you Kalamas, be you not misled by report or tradition or hearsay, be not misled by
proficiency in the scriptures, nor by mere logic or inference, nor after considering reasons,
nor after reflection on and approval of some theory, nor because it is becoming no out of respect
for a recluse, but Kalamas, when you know for yourselves, these things are unprofitable,
these things are blameworthy, these things are censured by the intelligent, these things went
perform and anti-taken, conduc to loss and sorrow, then do you reject them Kalamas, now what do
you think Kalamas can greed, loba, arises in a man, does it arise to his profit or to his loss,
now Kalamas does not this man, does become greedy being overcome by greed and losing control
of his mind, does he not kill a living creature, take what is not given, go after another's wife,
tell lies and lead another into such a state as causes his loss and sorrow for a long time,
and he said, he does so, sir, now what do you think Kalamas, but hatred, do some arises in a man,
does it arise to his profit or his loss, to his loss, now Kalamas does not this man, does become
hateful being overcome by hatred, losing control of his mind, does he not kill, does he not
take what is not given, commit adultery, tell lies, and lead another into such a state as causes
his loss and sorrow for a long time, and he replied, he does indeed, sir, and put and said,
now what do you think Kalamas, when delusioned Moha arises within a man, does it arise to his profit
or to his loss, and he says to his loss, and does not this man, does the loser, does he not
kill, etc, etc. He does, well then Kalamas, what do you think, are these things profitable or
unprofitable, unprofitable, sir, are they were blameworthy or not blameworthy, sir, are they essential
by the intelligent or not, they are essential, performed and undertaken, do they conduc to loss
and sorrow or not, they conduc to loss and sorrow, sir, it is just so we think, then the Buddha said,
so then Kalamas as to my words, which you just, to you just now, be you not, misled by report
or tradition or hearsay, be not misled by proficiency in the scriptures, nor by mere logic or
inference, nor after considering reasons, nor after reflection or in approval or some theory,
nor because it fits becoming nor out of respect or recluse, but Kalamas, when you know for yourself,
these things are profitable, these things are blameworthy, these things are essential by the wise,
these things, when performed and undertaken, conduc to loss and sorrow, then indeed, you reject
them, such was my reason for attracting those words. I just stop here to comment and hear the Buddha
is saying that those unprofitable things, blameworthy things, which lead to sorrow and which are
sensual by the wise, they should not follow them, but then unless we understand Dhamma, it is difficult
not to actually distinguish what is right and what is wrong, then the Buddha continued,
come now Kalamas, be you not misled by report or tradition or hearsay, etc,
but if at any time you know of yourselves, these things are profitable, they are blameless,
they are praised by the intelligent, when performed and undertaken, they conduc to profit and
happiness, then Kalamas, do you have not undertaken them, abide therein? Now what do you think,
Kalamas, when we read from read arises in a man, does it arise to his profit or to his loss,
to his profits? Does not this man not being greedy, not overcome by greed, having his mind under control,
does not he cease to kill, cease to steal, etc, he does. So in the same way, the Buddha talked
about read from read, read, delusion, then the Buddha said, are these things profitable on
profitable, they said profitable, are they blameworthy and not, they are not, etc, etc, etc. So the Buddha said,
in the same way, if they know of themselves, these things are profitable, they are conducive to
happiness, etc, then they should be practiced, and the Buddha continued. Now Kalamas,
he who is an Aryan disciple, read from coveting and malevolence, who is not bewildered by
self-control, then mind-cooled, with a heart possessed by good will, compassion, joy, equipment,
tears, etc. In this very life, he attains full comforts, first one, if there be a world beyond,
if there be fruit and ripening of teeth, then well or ill, then when body breaks up after that,
I shall be reborn in the happy lot in the heaven world. This is the first complete here,
teens. Second, if I ever, there be no world being, and the last three, you finish that
Sutan, number three, points, seven points, sixty-five, the famous Kalamas Uttar. And then I didn't,
to ask the end, I didn't have time to make some comments, and it's not just like to say,
that this Kalamas Uttar is one of the famous Suttar's. And the passage in the Suttar, which is
quite well known, is the one that says, be you not misled by report or tradition of hearsay,
be not misled by proficiency in the scriptures, nor by mere logic or inference,
nor after considering reasons, nor after reflection on an approval of some theory,
nor because it fits becoming, nor out of respect for a reclusive, but Kalamas.
And you know by yourselves, these things are unprofitable, these things are blameworthy,
these things are censured by the intelligent, these things went performed and undertaken,
confused to loss and sorrow, then indeed you reject them. And conversely, also for those things
that are profitable and not blameworthy and praised by no intelligent, etc.
So in this famous passage, the Buddha is actually giving advice to people, not only doing
this time, but even right up to today and even in the future, that whatever we come across,
that is taught, whether it is by tradition or what is heard or in the books or from logic,
from considering reasons and considering some theory, etc. We have to consider it very carefully,
whether those things that are taught, are profitable or unprofitable, whether they lead to
loss and sorrow or they lead to happiness. But then as I mentioned, the other thing is quite
difficult to actually judge whether something that is taught is correct or not. The only way
is to compare with other teachings, so it's very good to investigate a lot of investigation before we
actually conclude that some teaching is right or wrong. We have to take the trouble to compare
and then we can see that whatever is said here and said there, if they contradict each other,
then we have to think carefully and as I mentioned in some other sutras, if we have a clear mind,
then we can practice the only so monasikara, which is sorrow, consideration, and we have sorrow,
consideration, sorrow, attention, proper attention, proper consideration, then we can see things clearly,
and then also requires that we have a clear mind which is a developed mind and that this can be
cultivated from meditation. Now we come to a sutra number 3.7.607. The Buddha said,
monks, there are these three topics of discourse, what three? When we talk about pastime,
saying, does it worse in pastime? Or when we talk about future time, saying, does it will be in future
time? Or when we talk about the present time, saying, does it is now at present?
monks, it may be understood of a person by his conversation whether he is competing or incompetent to
discuss. Now monks, if this person, on being asked a question, does not give a categorical reply
to a question requiring it? Does not give a discriminating reply to a question requiring it? Does
not reply by a counter question to a question requiring it? And does not wave a question we should
be wave? Then monks, such a person is incompetent to discuss. But if this person, on being asked
these four sorts of questions, he is the proper reply, then he is competing to discuss.
Again, monks, it may be understood of a person by his conversation whether he is competing
or incompetent to discuss. If this person, on being asked a question, does not abide by conclusions
whether right or wrong? Does not abide by an assumption? Does not abide by recognized
arguments? Does not abide by usual procedure? In such case, monks, this person is incompetent to
discuss. But if he does all these, he is competent to discuss. Again, monks, it may be understood
of a person by his conversation whether he is competing or incompetent to discuss. If this person,
on being asked a question, he waves the question by another, or turns it off the point,
or displays of accession, values and softiness. In such case, monks is incompetent to discuss.
But if on the other hand, he does not have these things, he is competent. Yet again, monks,
it may be understood of a person by his conversation whether he is competing or incompetent to discuss
if on being asked a question, he loads with abuse and beats down the questioner,
laughs him to score and catches him up when he fought this. Such a one is incompetent to discuss.
But if he does none of these things, he is competent. monks, it may be understood of a person
by his conversation whether he is assured or unsure. He who lands not and he is unsure. He who lands
and he is a sure, he being a sure fully understands one thing, comprehends one thing,
abundance one thing, realizes one thing. So doing he reaches the perfect release, this
monks is a profit of top. This is the profit of deliberation of assurance of giving you to
advice. Namely, the liberation of mind without prospect. And this will time, you can see the Buddha,
he says that the first thing there, the first thing there, the first one is to answer a question.
Either you have to give a category, apply it sometimes or you have to give a discriminating reply.
And sometimes the question should be answered with a counter question. And sometimes
an nonsense question should be we should consider it at all. And also when you discuss or argue
with some person, you have to abide by conclusions, you have to abide by an assumption,
by recognize arguments, are you sure procedure. And some people who cannot discuss or cannot
argue, they sometimes evade a question when they should not evade it. And it turns it off the point
of show, malice, sockiness. And sometimes we have abusive or very strong food. And such kinds of
persons are not competing to discuss. So this is a guide to us sometimes. And when we discuss with
us, we are other people, how to talk. Now, the next two times 3.7.68, the Buddha said,
monks, if the wondrous of other views should thus question you, friends, there are these three
conditions, what three, last of passion and braga, hatred, dosa, delusion, moha, these are the
three. Now between these three, what is the distinction, what is the specific feature, what is the
difference? Thus question monks, how would you explain those wondrous of other views?
And the monks said, for as lot, things are rooted in the exalted one, have the exalted one for
their guide and resort. Well, for as lot, if the exalted one would reveal and to us the meaning of
this saying he has said, hearing the exalted one thought the monks will bear in mind. And the Buddha said,
then listen monks, apply your mind closely, I will speak, and the monks will play it even so
long. That's all that one said. Now, if the wondrous of other views should question you,
thus do you explain it? Preferences, last is slightly to be blamed, but it is slow to change.
hatred is much to be blamed, but it is quick to change. Delusion is much to be blamed, and it is slow to
change. I just stop here to comment. You notice here that last is said to be slightly to be blamed.
Once it is slightly to be blamed, because compared to the other two, it is not so harmful
in a sense, when you indulge in last and passion, you harm yourself, but not so much other people.
And whereas hatred, you can do a lot of harm to many people, and illusion, or illusion,
outer ignorance, and illusion, sometimes you do a lot of harm and you don't realize it.
So, last, it is slightly to be blamed, but it is very slow to change. Last is something that
all beings in the realm of sensual desire, and it is like a very strong tendency in all beings here.
hatred is much to be blamed, but quick to change. And delusion is much to be blamed, but it is slow to
change. So, you see, of all these three and delusion could be said to be the worst. It is very
harmful. And also, it is very slow to change delusion. Unless we take the trouble to change,
it is very difficult to change this. And the incentive, or less to take the trouble to change,
is suffering. And we are deluded, and then we suffer more. And when we suffer, then that is the
process to change ourselves. And then we try to investigate why we suffer. And then we study
the damba. Then we learn, we have to clear our mind, my meditation, and we learn to meditate, etc.
And then the Buddha said, what is the reason? What is the cause?
Let me see if there is a wondrous answer. What is the reason? What is the cause?
Why lust that has not a reason arises? Why lust that has a reason is liable to develop and grow?
It is the feature of attractiveness must be the reply. In him, who gives not thorough attention to the
feature of attractiveness, lust that has not a reason arises. And lust that has a reason is liable to
develop and grow. This is the reason, this is the cause. But sir, what is the reason? What is the
cause? Why hatred not yet a reason arises? Or if a reason is liable to develop and grow?
It is the feature of Lord Sameness or Equalityiness must be the reply. But in him, who gives
not thorough attention to the feature of Equalityiness, hatred arises. But sir, what is the reason?
Why delusion arises? It is, and thorough attention must be the reply. In him, who gives not
thorough attention delusion arises. Now, sir, what is the reason? What is the cause? Why lust
not yet a reason arises not? Or if a reason is abandoned? It is the feature of Equalityiness must be
the reply. In him, who gives thorough attention there to lust, not a reason arises not, or if a reason
is abandoned? But sir, what is the reason? Why hatred not yet a reason arises not? Or if a reason
is abandoned? It is the release of goodwill by mine must be the reply. In him, who gives thorough
attention to the release of goodwill by mine, both hatred that has not a reason arises not.
Or if a reason is abandoned? But sir, what is the reason? What is the cause? Why delusion?
There is not a reason arises not. Or if a reason is abandoned, thorough attention must be the reply.
In him, who practices thorough attention delusion, not a reason arises not. Or if a reason is abandoned?
So, sir, the Buddha said that lust arises, due to attractiveness, the feature of attractiveness.
If we see something is beautiful or attractive, be the greed arises and we lust for it. But
to reduce the lust, we have to see the repulsiveness in that thing. For example,
with the human body, the Buddha said that the attraction of the opposite sex is extremely strong.
So, to put the lust or the opposite sex, we practice contemplation of the 32 parts of the body,
which is meditation on the load sameness of the body or the repulsiveness of the body.
In that way, when we understand how the body is made up of all the different parts,
which are, in a way, an appropriate load sameness, then the attraction for the body is reduced.
And for hatred, hatred arises because of repulsiveness, we feel that something, like for example,
somebody sees something to us, if he is so repulsive as hatred arises. And the way to overcome hatred is
true meta. And that is good, we are loving kindness, we practice meditation on good, we are loving
kindness, we try to spread meta. Then our hatred or anger reduces a delusion arises from
antara consideration, antara consideration, and we can only get rid of delusion from practicing
antara consideration. And we see that antara consideration, the only so monasikara is important
for all these three things, lust, hatred, and delusion, lust, hatred, and delusion are known as
the three roots of unskillful action, a kushala-kamma, is because of lust, hatred, and delusion
that we live our lives very unskillfully. And when we live our lives unskillfully, we suffer more
than we ought to. There are certain types of suffering that can be avoided. If we live a skillful
life, but there are certain types of suffering that nobody can avoid. For example, the body
growing old body, getting sick, the body dying, those that cannot be avoided. But there are
other types of suffering that we can be avoided. For example, we drink and drive, we drink and drive,
then we get an accident, and that is unnecessary. If we had the skill, not to drink and drive,
say you drink, you do not drive, you drive, you do not drink, then we would do what we would
then unnecessary suffering.
